UK unemployment fell to 4.9% in the three months to April while wage growth including bonuses climbed to 4.4%, with vacancies slumping to their lowest level since the three months to April 2021 at 707,000, according to ONS figures.

Amazon founder Jeff Bezos told VivaTech Paris that AI will create "a labour shortage" rather than mass redundancies, in direct contradiction of figures like former UK prime minister Rishi Sunak who has said AI is already affecting young people's job prospects.

Elastic has agreed to buy AI bug-fixing startup DeductiveAI for up to $85 million; Deductive was founded in 2023 and had raised a $7.5 million seed round led by CRV at a $33 million valuation.

Russia's Kremlin aide Yuri Ushakov said Moscow was expecting the return of White House envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner to Moscow soon for Ukraine talks, even as dates had not yet been determined.

The UK's information commissioner John Edwards resigned after a workplace investigation concluded "there was a case to answer" and that his behaviour "fell short of the conduct expected from a public official"; Science Secretary Liz Kendall said she had seen evidence of "vulgar and highly sexualised language" used in interactions with staff.

The US government forced Anthropic to pull its two newest models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, citing national security concerns after Amazon researchers allegedly found a way to bypass Fable 5's guardrails, with cybersecurity researchers signing an open letter calling the move dangerous.

The BBC was given rare access to Israeli-occupied southern Lebanon as part of a humanitarian convoy distributing aid to Christian villages, where mainly Shia villages have been completely destroyed by Israeli air strikes or demolitions, with human rights groups saying some of what happened there amounts to the deliberate destruction of civilian infrastructure.
